1: A revelation of God: Psalm 76 is an Asaphite Zion Psalm celebrating YHWH's defeat of an unnamed enemy army (possibly the Assyrian siege of 701 BC, Isaiah 36-37). "God is known in Judah; his name is great in Israel." The particularity of divine self-revelation — in Judah, in Israel — is the covenant specificity that the NT takes up and universalizes in Christ. Romans 3:2: "much in every way! First of all, the Jews have been entrusted with the very words of God." The knowledge of God that begins in Judah and Israel reaches all nations through Christ (John 4:22: "salvation is from the Jews").
2: A direct connection: "His tabernacle is in Salem; his dwelling place is in Zion." Salem (šālēm) = Jerusalem, but also the Hebrew word for peace/wholeness. Genesis 14:18 records that Melchizedek was king of Salem — king of peace. Hebrews 7:2 makes the connection explicit: "First, the name Melchizedek means 'king of righteousness'; then also, 'king of Salem' means 'king of peace.'" YHWH's dwelling in Salem in Psalms 76:2 is the pre-figuration of Christ as the Melchizedekian priest-king of Salem — the one whose priesthood is permanent because he is "king of peace" in the eternal Salem (Hebrews 12:22: the heavenly Jerusalem).
3: A direct connection: "There he shattered the flashing arrows, the shield, the sword, and all the weapons of war." The divine warrior breaking all weapons at Zion is the type of Christ's disarmament of spiritual powers at the cross. Colossians 2:15: "And having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross." The weapons of sin, death, and the accusation of the law — the arrows, shields, and swords of the spiritual realm — are shattered by the cross at the true Zion. What Psalms 76:3 describes as a military victory is the cross's spiritual-warfare victory in which all weapons against the community of Christ are broken.
4: A revelation of God: "You are glorious and majestic, more than the everlasting mountains of plunder." The incomparable divine glory — exceeding the most permanent and impressive human accumulation — is the foundation of doxology. 1 Timothy 6:15-16: "God, the blessed and only Ruler, the King of kings and Lord of lords, who alone is immortal and who lives in unapproachable light... to him be honor and might forever." In Christ, this incomparable divine glory becomes visible (John 1:14: "we have seen his glory, the glory of the one and only Son"). The majestic glory of Psalms 76:4 is the glory beheld in the face of Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6).
5: A shadow: "The valiant warriors have been stripped of their plunder; they have fallen into the sleep of death; not one soldier could lift a hand." The defeat of the mighty — valiant warriors sleeping in death, hands unable to rise — is the pattern of eschatological judgment on all human power that opposes YHWH. 1 Corinthians 15:25-26: "For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death." The sleep of death that overcomes the mighty warriors of Psalms 76:5 is the condition that Christ's resurrection begins to reverse — death itself falling into the sleep from which it will never wake (Revelation 20:14: "death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire").
6: A shadow: "At your rebuke, O God of Jacob, both chariot and horse fell into a dead sleep." The divine rebuke rendering the war-machine (chariot and horse, the symbols of military might from Egypt to Assyria) inert — a "dead sleep" — is the pattern of the Exodus (Exodus 14:25: "He jammed the wheels of their chariots"). Christ rebukes the storm (Mark 4:39: "Quiet! Be still!") — demonstrating the authority to "rebuke" the forces of chaos and power that only YHWH possesses. The chariot-silencing of Psalms 76:6 is a type of Christ's authority over every force that appears overwhelming to human beings.
7: A revelation of God: "But you — you alone are to be feared. Who can stand before you when your anger is kindled?" The divine uniqueness in terror — YHWH alone worthy of ultimate fear — is the foundation of NT awe before the risen Christ. Revelation 6:15-17: "Then the kings of the earth, the princes, the generals, the rich, the mighty, and everyone else, both slave and free, hid in caves... 'Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who sits on the throne and from the wrath of the Lamb! For the great day of their wrath has come, and who can withstand it?'" The "who can stand?" of Psalms 76:7 is the question of Revelation's judgment: none can stand before the wrath of the Lamb.
8: A revelation of God: "From heaven you pronounced judgment; the earth feared and stood still." The heavenly origin of divine judgment — and the earth's arrested response — is the imagery of the last judgment. Matthew 24:30: "Then will appear the sign of the Son of Man in heaven. And then all the peoples of the earth will mourn when they see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven, with power and great glory." The earth that "feared and stood still" at YHWH's heavenly judgment in Psalms 76:8 is the earth that mourns and stands still at the Parousia of Christ — the divine judgment from heaven enacted through the Son of Man.
9: A revelation of God: "when God arose to judge, to save all the humble of the earth." The divine arising — YHWH rousing himself to act — for the dual purpose of judgment and salvation is the resurrection's shape. The resurrection is the divine "arising" that simultaneously judges (vindicating the innocent, condemning the conspirators) and saves (the humble who trust in the risen one). Luke 18:14: "everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted." The "humble of the earth" who are saved when God arises are those who entrust themselves to the one God raised — the humble who inherit the eschatological salvation.
10: A revelation of God: "Surely human fury will bring you praise; whatever wrath remains you will restrain." The divine sovereignty over human anger — even wrath brings YHWH praise, even the remaining wrath is restrained — is the paradox of the cross. Acts 4:27-28: "they did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen." The human fury of the Passion — Judas's betrayal, the Sanhedrin's conspiracy, Pilate's capitulation, the crowd's violence — all brought YHWH praise through the resurrection. The wrath of human beings against the Messiah became the instrument of their salvation (1 Corinthians 2:8: "if they had known, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory").
11: A revelation of God: "Make vows to the LORD your God and keep them; let all the surrounding nations bring tribute to the one who is to be feared." The call for all nations to bring tribute (šay) to YHWH is the universal-worship vision of the Psalter (cf. Psalms 2:8; Psalms 22:27-28; Psalms 72:10-11). Philippians 2:10-11: "that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord." The surrounding nations bringing tribute to the fearsome God of Psalms 76:11 is the eschatological worship of all nations before Christ — the fulfillment of the universal-tribute vision in the risen Lord who bears the divine name.
12: A revelation of God: "He cuts off the spirit of princes; he is fearsome to the kings of the earth." The divine authority over all human royalty — "cutting off the spirit" of rulers — is YHWH's absolute sovereignty. Revelation 1:5: "Jesus Christ, who is the faithful witness, the firstborn from the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth." Christ is not merely subject to kings — he is their ruler. The "fearsome to the kings" of Psalms 76:12 is the eschatological reality: every earthly king stands under the authority of the one who is "ruler of the kings of the earth," whose kingdom "will never end" (Luke 1:33).
